essence: David scopes a comparative tag-ontology experiment: run three separate tests across different brains, wanting upfront clarity on input/output formats, judging criteria, and concrete example ontology files, noting ontologies apply to groups of brains rather than single brains.

actions.text: Define the three test brains, ontology example files, and judging criteria before running the multi-brain tag tests
Investigate statusline bug showing 5% instead of 75% for seven-day usage

essence: David configures multi-model local LLM hosting across his Macs (Ollama on M4 Pro exposed via Tailscale with OLLAMA_HOST=0.0.0.0, background service surviving reboot), pulls models (Phi-4, Gemma 3, Llama 3, Qwen 2.5), and demands documentation of the fallback mechanism and affinity-groups registry plus a benchmarking test plan across five brains.

actions.text: Document fallback mechanism, affinity groups registry, and tagging scheme; commit, push, and hand over
Run Ollama as a background service bound to 0.0.0.0 so the M4 Mini can reach it over Tailscale after reboot
Pull Phi-4 14B, Gemma 3 12B, Llama 3 8B, Qwen 2.5 and present a 4-5 prompt test plan across the five target brains

essence: David asks whether the 'appydave' model alias should be renamed to 'answerwell' in the model playbook, wants support extended to the M4 Pro/Mini and M2, and a report of models blocked by OpenRouter issues.

actions.text: Update the model playbook, decide on appydave-to-answerwell rename, and list OpenRouter-blocked models

essence: David approves the agent's chosen execution path for testing tag ontologies against three files across five brains with background agents, noting llama-backed agents cannot run in parallel and that overflow tags are promotion candidates, but holds the kickoff until he is ready.

actions.text: Kick off background-agent tag ontology execution (serialised where agents share the llama model) on David's go signal

essence: Facing Lars questioning his $5K/month spend after a first month with no tangible outcome, David sets a delivery roadmap: AngelEye wave-based agent workflow visualization built over the weekend, app auto-update, drip-fed education via an automated blog, and teaching Lars to build custom agent teams with the BMAD Module Builder.

actions.text: Build the agent-workflow wave visualization over the weekend and enable app auto-update for Lars
Automate David's blog-creation workflow using BMAD Module Builder as the teaching example for Lars

essence: David specifies the AngelEye 'wave' visualization for multi-agent BMAD story workflows (Bob/Amelia/Nate/Taylor/Lisa plus Sentinel, Advisor, Relay): horizontal agent wave with ghosted future steps and backtrack lines, vertical chat transcript, agent profile panel, right-hand audit log of predicates/observations/classifications/extractions, with copy-button handovers, JSON document structures, and Mochaccino mocks validated on story 2.4 then 2.5 and the epic-2 retrospective.

actions.text: Compile the wave visualization requirements into a design document, define JSON structures and agent metadata profiles, and build 3-4 Mochaccino mocks from story 2.4 data
Write a handover of the visualization work for the AngelEye brain

essence: Chiang Mai AI builders meetup covers Claude Dispatch/computer-control/auto-mode, plugins as distribution containers for related skills (validating David's consultant-pattern packaging question), git-backed dev folders with a gitignored upstreams folder and locations.json (David's pattern), Anthropic's 5-hour limit reductions pushing load into Asian hours, and a plan to move next week's meetup online if AQI stays hazardous.

Meetup lightning talk on rolling out Claude Code in a regulated company (Ansible installer, AWS LLM proxy, marketplace-JSON plugin distribution, markdown-in-git knowledge base with lazy-loaded claude.md files); David contributes his 5-brains/5-machines pattern, Syncthing peer-to-peer dropboxes for semi-transient shared data, and ALS-style attribute-based access control enforced via write-blocking hooks.

Duplicate capture of the same conversation: David approves the execution path for tag-ontology tests over three files across five brains with background agents (llama-backed agents serialised), registers overflow tags as promotion candidates, and defers kickoff until ready.

David and Joy plan a juice bar menu system: bilingual (English/Thai) data structures and micro-app entry forms capturing ingredients, seasonality, cost, juice yield, stock, and recipes, to compute cost-per-drink and margins, group drinks into families with catchy names, and drive menu design and marketing.

actions.text: Define ingredient/recipe schemas and bilingual Thai data-entry forms persisting to the file system

essence: Pre-meetup chat where David articulates his three-layer memory model (canonical source of truth, synthesized outcome, provenance/citation chain for reconstruction) against another builder's semantic-vs-episodic Postgres memory with time-aware ranking, then the group reviews Claude Code scheduled tasks versus Codex app scheduling and session-based /loop constraints.

Saturday meetup on token economics: reflection/analytics agents beat autonomous content agents, format compression (CSV/parakeet/Vercel markdown), off-peak double quotas favouring Chiang Mai hours, expected death of subsidised $200 unlimited plans, Codex CSV fan-out sub-agents, Jensen Huang's token-burn-as-skill claim, and Ralph-Wiggum-style auto-improving skill loops David explains to the group.

David flags that OMI conversations captured on one machine are missing on another (same recurring gap as app.json/locations.json shared-knowledge problem, parked for now) and sets a display standard: OMI file times always show Bangkok first, then UTC.

actions.text: Adopt Bangkok-first-then-UTC timestamp display for all OMI transcript listings

essence: David plans repositioning appydave.com around the agentic operating system: refresh brand identity docs, build a flexible content system that dynamically creates pages and turns every video across Clawding Lab, AITLDR, and AppyDave into mini-blogs, with paid contact, email list, Skool enrollment, and video discovery, starting with deep research on sites doing this well.

actions.text: Deep research websites that dynamically generate structured content pages from videos and run monetized contact flows
